summary: DeVault argues that contributors to open source projects should never sign Contributor License Agreements (CLAs), which grant project maintainers rights beyond what the open source license itself provides. He contends that CLAs undermine the equal relationship between contributors and maintainers by giving maintainers the power to relicense contributed work under restrictive or proprietary terms. He cites the Redis Labs incident as a concrete example of CLA abuse, where community contributions were pulled into a nonfree product. DeVault endorses the Linux kernel's Developer Certificate of Origin as a better alternative and advises contributors to simply fork projects rather than sign away their rights.
key_points: CLAs give project maintainers the ability to relicense contributor work under more restrictive licenses, up to fully proprietary
Open source licenses create an equal relationship between maintainers, users, and contributors that CLAs deliberately disrupt
The Redis Labs relicensing under the Anti-Commons Clause demonstrates real-world CLA abuse against community contributors
Contributors can protect themselves by forking projects instead of signing CLAs, a right guaranteed by every open source license
The Linux kernel's Developer Certificate of Origin is a good alternative that covers legal bases without stripping contributor rights
money_quotes: A CLA is a kick in the groin to a contributor's good-faith contribution to the project.

What the CLA is actually used for is to give the project maintainers the ability to relicense your work under a more restrictive software license, up to and including making it entirely closed source.
Open source is a commitment to your community. Once you make it, you cannot take it back.
You don't get the benefits associated with being an open source project if you have an exit hatch.
Making it open source is explicitly waiving that right.
When you send a patch to a project, you are affording them the same rights they afforded you. The relationship is one of equals.
When you sign a CLA, you give them unequal power over you.
Call me petty, but I can't in good faith call it the 'Commons Clause' when its purpose is to remove software from the commons.
key_insight: Signing a CLA breaks the fundamental equality that open source licenses establish between contributors and maintainers, enabling the very proprietary lock-in that open source exists to prevent.
